abstract.text: This article describes the KeyboardKit action engine.

inlineContent.text: The
type is a central concept in KeyboardKit, where an action can be triggered by the keyboard or by code, then handled with a
A
can either handle a
in a standard way, or handle it based on a certain
for more granular control when triggering actions programatically.
injects more capabilities into the action handler, to make it handle locale-specific input methods like Vietnamese.
KeyboardKit has a
enum that defines a bunch of keyboard-related actions. It’s also a namespace for action-related types and views, like a
that provides you with a standard way to handle actions.
Here are just some of the many actions that the enum defines. See the full
documentation for a full list of actions:
- triggers a backspace deletion.
- inserts a character.
- dismisses the keyboard.
- inserts a diacritic.
- inserts an emoji.
- changes the keyboard type.
- triggers the system keyboard switcher.
- triggers the locale switcher.
Many actions have a
, and a
or
while others require manual handling by creating a custom
A
can handle actions, apply
values, trigger audio & haptic feedback, etc. It’s in the center of how actions are handled, and can be customized to fit your needs.
For instance, to customize what happens when a user double-taps space, you can override
and check if the action is
and the gesture is
. If so, do your custom thing, else call super.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of
and injects it into
. You can replace it at any time, as described further down.
injects more capabilities into the
, to make it handle locale-specific input methods like Vietnamese TELEX, VIQR, and VNI, and handle other locale and pro-specific replacements.
KeyboardKit automatically triggers
s when users interact with the keyboard or when certain events happen. Actions are by default handled with the main
, which you can replace to customize how actions are handled.
You can trigger actions programmatically with
, or trigger
to simulate a certain action gesture:
Keyboard actions can also be triggered by a
or by applying a
modifier to any view:
applies this modifier to all its buttons, to automatically make them support press, release, long press, repeat, etc.
Actions are by default handled with the main
, which you can replace to customize how actions are handled. This is required for some actions that don’t have a standard or default behavior.
You can either implement the
protocol from scratch, or inherit the
class and override any of the many functions that it provides to add your own customizations to the standard behavior.
A
can also handle
values, which are for instance what happens when a user taps a suggestion in an

code: func handle(_ suggestion: Autocomplete.Suggestion, with handler: KeyboardActionHandler) {

inlineContent.text: This will by default insert the suggestion into the text document proxy. You can customize this behavior with a custom action handler.
You can create a custom
to customize how certain actions are handled, and to handle actions that don’t have a standard or default behavior, like
, etc.
You can implement the

To use your custom handler instead of the standard one, just inject it into
by replacing its
property:
This will make KeyboardKit use your custom action handler instead of the standard one.

used perform autocomplete as the user types.
